notes.anchor: a hidden and safe road to the Upper Sea
notes.note: The Upper Sea (mare superum) is the Adriatic, on Italy's eastern coast, from which Cicero would have to embark to reach Pompey in Greece; the Lower Sea is the Tyrrhenian to the west. He complains he has no safe overland route to a usable Adriatic port at this season.

notes.anchor: they were making vows for him in his sickness
notes.note: In the summer of 50 BC Pompey fell gravely ill at Naples and recovered, and the towns of Italy made public vows and held thanksgivings for him; Cicero bitterly contrasts that devotion with their present rush to Caesar. The episode had been read as proof of Pompey's popularity.

Peisistratus was the sixth-century Athenian who seized sole power as tyrant; Cicero casts Caesar in the role, a usurper whose mildness only makes his domination more palatable. The comparison brands Caesar's clemency as the velvet glove of tyranny.

notes.anchor: delighted by this man's insidious clemency
notes.note: Caesar's policy of sparing his defeated opponents (clementia), shown at Corfinium, won him goodwill across Italy; Cicero calls it insidiosa, a trap that disarms resistance more effectively than terror. The judgement anticipates the later view of the clementia Caesaris as a tool of autocracy.

notes.anchor: The jurors out of the three hundred and sixty
notes.note: The standing criminal courts at Rome drew jurors from a panel of 360 (120 each from senators, knights, and tribuni aerarii under the Lex Aurelia); the propertied men who staffed them had favoured Pompey. Cicero says even these now shudder at the threat of a Pompeian return.

Luceria in Apulia was Pompey's headquarters during the retreat, from which he was rumoured to be issuing Sullan-style threats of proscription against those who stayed behind; "Lucerias" stands for those menacing pronouncements. The jurors dread a victorious Pompey more than a victorious Caesar.
